summary: Stancil argues that despite sweeping technological advances, productivity hasn’t improved because the tools we use shape behavior more than any process or best-practice talk. Slack exemplifies this by incentivizing frantic, low-quality communication, so the real path to better work is to build products that enforce better processes, especially for data teams. In data, he claims only purpose-built tools will shift norms around requests, alerting, and decision-making.
money_quotes: Slack is a kanban board with two stages: Gone forever, and do it now.

But if we score Slack against communication tools’ more meaningful measures—does it help us make good decisions, and keep everyone informed of and aligned around those decisions?—Slack is a spectacular step backwards.
If an environment makes a particular behavior convenient, that’s what we’ll do.
No matter how much we bang on process, we won’t solve our own productivity paradox until we get the products right.
To butcher every self-styled internet intellectual’s favorite quote, “the product is the process.”
key_insight: Lasting improvements in how data teams work will come from purpose-built products that reshape behavior, not from exhortations about process.
